item.title: ERDNet: Efficient Ship Object Detection in Haze Environment
item.dc:creator: Yu Zhang
Pengcheng Guo
Wenqiang Li
Moran Ju
Qing Hu
item.description: Ship object detection faces the challenge of increasing the difficulty of positioning in hazy environments. Additionally, the latest convolutional neural network (CNN) cannot obtain satisfactory detection results. Therefore, we propose ERDNet, a dual-branch-driven end-to-end network, to improve ship detection accuracy during hazy weather. Specifically, we design a two-branch feature extraction network through complementary attentional fusion to enhance the object feature information of low-quality images. Second, we designed a feature pyramid fusion structure called ERPSA-PAN to aggregate context information effectively. ERPSA-PAN improves the feature fusion capability of the model by suppressing background interference and enhancing useful information. In addition, the spatial-frequency fusion block (SFFB) module with expanded receptive fields is added to the ERDNet detection head to improve the detection ability for multiscale targets. More importantly, we design a robust haze loss to handle different degrees of haze. We introduce two new haze ship datasets, Hazy-SeaShips and Hazy-Boats, which include 17,000 synthetic haze images and 2898 real haze images, respectively, to address the lack of hazy ship image datasets. The images cover variations such as haze thicknesses, ship types, and scales, along with complex backgrounds, and occlusions. The experimental results show that the proposed method is superior to other state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods and achieves relatively competitive results. The source codes, and datasets are available on https://github.com/ZikHH/ERDNet. Published: 2026-04-21T19:51:35+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Zhang; Pengcheng Guo; Wenqiang Li; Moran Ju; Qing Hu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3684409

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Ship object detection faces the challenge of increasing the difficulty of positioning in hazy environments. Additionally, the latest convolutional neural network (CNN) cannot obtain satisfactory detection results. Therefore, we propose ERDNet, a dual-branch-driven end-to-end network, to improve ship detection accuracy during hazy weather. Specifically, we design a two-branch feature extraction network through complementary attentional fusion to enhance the object feature information of low-quality images. Second, we designed a feature pyramid fusion structure called ERPSA-PAN to aggregate context information effectively. ERPSA-PAN improves the feature fusion capability of the model by suppressing background interference and enhancing useful information. In addition, the spatial-frequency fusion block (SFFB) module with expanded receptive fields is added to the ERDNet detection head to improve the detection ability for multiscale targets. More importantly, we design a robust haze loss to handle different degrees of haze. We introduce two new haze ship datasets, Hazy-SeaShips and Hazy-Boats, which include 17,000 synthetic haze images and 2898 real haze images, respectively, to address the lack of hazy ship image datasets. The images cover variations such as haze thicknesses, ship types, and scales, along with complex backgrounds, and occlusions. The experimental results show that the proposed method is superior to other state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods and achieves relatively competitive results. The source codes, and datasets are available on https://github.com/ZikHH/ERDNet.

item.title: Linear attention goes global in molecular dynamics
item.dc:creator: Sheng Gong
Wen Yan
item.description: A new attention mechanism brings long-range awareness to machine learning force fields with linear cost and preservation of symmetry. The method offers a flexible alternative to existing long-range modules, including fragmentation-based interactions and physics-based long-range fixes. Published: 2026-04-21T09:03:21+00:00 Venue: Nature Machine Intelligence Score: 0.801 (must_read)

Authors: Sheng Gong; Wen Yan

Journal: Nature Machine Intelligence

A new attention mechanism brings long-range awareness to machine learning force fields with linear cost and preservation of symmetry. The method offers a flexible alternative to existing long-range modules, including fragmentation-based interactions and physics-based long-range fixes.

item.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Detection Transformers: Object-Level Calibration and Image-Level Reliability
item.dc:creator: Young-Jin Park
Carson Sobolewski
Navid Azizan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: DEtection TRansformer (DETR) and its variants have emerged as promising architectures for object detection, offering an end-to-end prediction pipeline. In practice, however, DETRs generate hundreds of predictions that far outnumber the actual objects present in an image. This raises a critical question: which of these predictions could be trusted? This is particularly important for safety-critical applications, such as in autonomous vehicles. Addressing this concern, we provide empirical and theoretical evidence that predictions within the same image play distinct roles, resulting in varying reliability levels. Our analysis reveals that DETRs employ an optimal “specialist strategy”: one prediction per object is trained to be well-calibrated , while the remaining predictions are trained to suppress their foreground confidence to near zero, even when maintaining accurate localization. We show that this strategy emerges as the loss-minimizing solution to the Hungarian matching algorithm, fundamentally shaping DETRs' outputs. While selecting the well-calibrated predictions is ideal, they are unidentifiable at inference time. This means that any post-processing algorithm—used to identify trustworthy predictions—poses a risk of outputting a set of predictions with mixed calibration levels. Therefore, practical deployment necessitates a joint evaluation of both the model's calibration quality and the effectiveness of the post-processing algorithm. However, we demonstrate that existing metrics like average precision and expected calibration error are inadequate for this task. To address this issue, we further introduce Object-level Calibration Error (OCE), which evaluates calibration by aggregating predictions per ground-truth object rather than per prediction. This object-centric design penalizes both retaining suppressed predictions and missed ground truth foreground objects, making OCE suitable for both evaluating models and identifying reliable prediction subsets. Final... Published: 2026-04-22T19:49:36+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.797 (must_read)

Authors: Young-Jin Park; Carson Sobolewski; Navid Azizan

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3686715

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

DEtection TRansformer (DETR) and its variants have emerged as promising architectures for object detection, offering an end-to-end prediction pipeline. In practice, however, DETRs generate hundreds of predictions that far outnumber the actual objects present in an image. This raises a critical question: which of these predictions could be trusted? This is particularly important for safety-critical applications, such as in autonomous vehicles. Addressing this concern, we provide empirical and theoretical evidence that predictions within the same image play distinct roles, resulting in varying reliability levels. Our analysis reveals that DETRs employ an optimal “specialist strategy”: one prediction per object is trained to be well-calibrated , while the remaining predictions are trained to suppress their foreground confidence to near zero, even when maintaining accurate localization. We show that this strategy emerges as the loss-minimizing solution to the Hungarian matching algorithm, fundamentally shaping DETRs' outputs. While selecting the well-calibrated predictions is ideal, they are unidentifiable at inference time. This means that any post-processing algorithm—used to identify trustworthy predictions—poses a risk of outputting a set of predictions with mixed calibration levels. Therefore, practical deployment necessitates a joint evaluation of both the model's calibration quality and the effectiveness of the post-processing algorithm. However, we demonstrate that existing metrics like average precision and expected calibration error are inadequate for this task. To address this issue, we further introduce Object-level Calibration Error (OCE), which evaluates calibration by aggregating predictions per ground-truth object rather than per prediction. This object-centric design penalizes both retaining suppressed predictions and missed ground truth foreground objects, making OCE suitable for both evaluating models and identifying reliable prediction subsets. Final...

item.title: Counterfactual Risk Minimization for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
item.dc:creator: Yanhua Yang
Muli Yang
Jiahua Li
Henan Wang
Cheng Deng
Hongyuan Zhu
item.description: The out-of-distribution (OOD) property in data is deemed as one main challenge hindering the generalization ability of machine learning algorithms. However, the underlying reasons for this property remain an intriguing and open question that has yet to be fully understood. In this paper, we seek to enhance our understanding of the OOD phenomenon by framing it as a problem of distribution shift and addressing it through two complementary causal perspectives. The first is a generative causal view that elucidates the data generation process. We introduce a novel three-dimensional coordinate system to represent three fundamental distribution shifts, illustrating their role in various OOD generalization problems. The second is an anti-causal view that focuses on the model learning process. We develop an effective approach dubbed Counterfactual Risk Minimization (CRM) to address arbitrary distribution shifts in a unified framework. Additionally, we introduce a new multidomain visual recognition dataset called CONA to facilitate further exploration of OOD generalization. We conduct evaluations of CRM alongside several state-of-the-art competitors on four benchmark datasets under the three distribution shifts. The results not only affirm CRM’s superiority but also shed light on potential future directions. Published: 2026-04-22T19:52:39+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanhua Yang; Muli Yang; Jiahua Li; Henan Wang; Cheng Deng; Hongyuan Zhu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3683291

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

The out-of-distribution (OOD) property in data is deemed as one main challenge hindering the generalization ability of machine learning algorithms. However, the underlying reasons for this property remain an intriguing and open question that has yet to be fully understood. In this paper, we seek to enhance our understanding of the OOD phenomenon by framing it as a problem of distribution shift and addressing it through two complementary causal perspectives. The first is a generative causal view that elucidates the data generation process. We introduce a novel three-dimensional coordinate system to represent three fundamental distribution shifts, illustrating their role in various OOD generalization problems. The second is an anti-causal view that focuses on the model learning process. We develop an effective approach dubbed Counterfactual Risk Minimization (CRM) to address arbitrary distribution shifts in a unified framework. Additionally, we introduce a new multidomain visual recognition dataset called CONA to facilitate further exploration of OOD generalization. We conduct evaluations of CRM alongside several state-of-the-art competitors on four benchmark datasets under the three distribution shifts. The results not only affirm CRM’s superiority but also shed light on potential future directions.

item.title: FHNet: Frequency Guided Hierarchical Optimization Network for Few-Shot Fine-Grained Classification of Spatially Dispersed and Aggregated Data
item.dc:creator: YuXiang Wang
Xinyu Dong
Xiaoyang Wan
Shiming Chen
Yongbin Qin
Wu Liu
Yong-Jin Liu
Jiebo Luo
Qi Wang
item.description: The core challenge of Few-Shot Fine-Grained Image Classification (FS-FGIC) lies in accurately capturing discriminative representations from limited samples. However, conventional spatial-domain approaches rely heavily on localized saliency, creating a structural blind spot for scenarios where discriminative cues are spatially dispersed and non-contiguously distributed. We observe that while attention-based variants offer theoretical global modeling, their over-parameterized attention maps suffer from high data dependency and optimization volatility under few-shot regimes. Without sufficient data to regularize the attention manifold, these models often collapse onto spurious spatial correlations or background noise, failing to establish stable structural priors for scattered cues. To bridge this gap and simulate these practical challenges, we first construct the Feature-Dispersed Plant Disease Classification (FD-PDC) dataset, providing a rigorous benchmark where features lack definitive focal points. From a methodological perspective, we propose the Frequency-guided Hierarchical Optimization Network (FHNet) to reconcile the limitations of spatial perception by leveraging coordinate-agnostic spectral signatures. FHNet integrates dual-frequency interaction, hierarchical alignment, and support-query mutual learning to transform stochastic spatial fragments into stable frequency-domain representations. This synergy effectively captures non-contiguous textures and regularizes intra-class variance, resolving the optimization hurdles inherent in scattered features. Extensive experiments on both traditional aggregated benchmarks (CUB, Dogs, Cars) and our dispersed FD-PDC dataset demonstrate that FHNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Our results validate the superiority of frequency-guided perception in bridging the gap between localized spatial bias and global feature dispersion. Codes are available at: https://github.com/GZU-SAMLab/FHNet . Published: 2026-04-22T16:08:53+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: YuXiang Wang; Xinyu Dong; Xiaoyang Wan; Shiming Chen; Yongbin Qin; Wu Liu; Yong-Jin Liu; Jiebo Luo; Qi Wang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104379

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

The core challenge of Few-Shot Fine-Grained Image Classification (FS-FGIC) lies in accurately capturing discriminative representations from limited samples. However, conventional spatial-domain approaches rely heavily on localized saliency, creating a structural blind spot for scenarios where discriminative cues are spatially dispersed and non-contiguously distributed. We observe that while attention-based variants offer theoretical global modeling, their over-parameterized attention maps suffer from high data dependency and optimization volatility under few-shot regimes. Without sufficient data to regularize the attention manifold, these models often collapse onto spurious spatial correlations or background noise, failing to establish stable structural priors for scattered cues. To bridge this gap and simulate these practical challenges, we first construct the Feature-Dispersed Plant Disease Classification (FD-PDC) dataset, providing a rigorous benchmark where features lack definitive focal points. From a methodological perspective, we propose the Frequency-guided Hierarchical Optimization Network (FHNet) to reconcile the limitations of spatial perception by leveraging coordinate-agnostic spectral signatures. FHNet integrates dual-frequency interaction, hierarchical alignment, and support-query mutual learning to transform stochastic spatial fragments into stable frequency-domain representations. This synergy effectively captures non-contiguous textures and regularizes intra-class variance, resolving the optimization hurdles inherent in scattered features. Extensive experiments on both traditional aggregated benchmarks (CUB, Dogs, Cars) and our dispersed FD-PDC dataset demonstrate that FHNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Our results validate the superiority of frequency-guided perception in bridging the gap between localized spatial bias and global feature dispersion. Codes are available at: https://github.com/GZU-SAMLab/FHNet .

item.title: Box-Supervised 3D Instance Segmentation with Level Set Evolution and Cross-View Consistency
item.dc:creator: He Wang
Guofeng Zhang
item.description: Weakly supervised 3D instance segmentation aims to reduce the high cost of point-wise annotations while maintaining competitive accuracy compared with fully supervised methods. Among various weak annotations, box annotation offers an ideal trade-off between labeling efficiency and supervision strength. However, most box-supervised methods rely on a two-stage training pipeline: 1) generating pseudo-labels, 2) training the segmentation model with pseudo-labels, which is iterative and sensitive to pseudo-label quality. To address this issue, we propose an end-to-end framework that directly learns instance masks from box annotations without an explicit pseudo-label generation and iterative relabeling and retraining stage. Specifically, we introduce a boundary-aware refinement module that adaptively learns instance boundaries from boxes through level set evolution. Furthermore, we propose a multi-scale geometric augmentation module to alleviate semantic ambiguity in overlapping regions by applying cross-view consistency constraints on predictions. Finally, we construct a multi-objective optimization framework, which improves both the training stability of level-set-evolution-based boundary refinement and the overall segmentation performance. Extensive experimental results on both indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate that our method achieves the SOTA performance across multiple benchmark datasets with different backbones and closely approaches fully supervised counterparts. Published: 2026-04-21T19:51:35+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: He Wang; Guofeng Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3684400

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Weakly supervised 3D instance segmentation aims to reduce the high cost of point-wise annotations while maintaining competitive accuracy compared with fully supervised methods. Among various weak annotations, box annotation offers an ideal trade-off between labeling efficiency and supervision strength. However, most box-supervised methods rely on a two-stage training pipeline: 1) generating pseudo-labels, 2) training the segmentation model with pseudo-labels, which is iterative and sensitive to pseudo-label quality. To address this issue, we propose an end-to-end framework that directly learns instance masks from box annotations without an explicit pseudo-label generation and iterative relabeling and retraining stage. Specifically, we introduce a boundary-aware refinement module that adaptively learns instance boundaries from boxes through level set evolution. Furthermore, we propose a multi-scale geometric augmentation module to alleviate semantic ambiguity in overlapping regions by applying cross-view consistency constraints on predictions. Finally, we construct a multi-objective optimization framework, which improves both the training stability of level-set-evolution-based boundary refinement and the overall segmentation performance. Extensive experimental results on both indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate that our method achieves the SOTA performance across multiple benchmark datasets with different backbones and closely approaches fully supervised counterparts.

item.title: RCACF: 4D Radar-Camera Adaptive and Cooperative Fusion for 3D detection
item.dc:creator: Shihao Wang
Keyan Ren
Wenzhuo Zhu
Jingyi Huang
Jianyu Gu
Qing Niu
item.description: Fusing 4D radar and camera data shows greater potential for 3D object detection in autonomous driving systems. However, existing approaches are constrained by static fusion rules and isolated modality processing, leading to limited environmental and inadequate cross-modal complementarity. In an attempt to tackle these problems, we introduce a fast yet effective framework for adaptive and cooperative fusion 4D radar-camera fusion called RCACF. The framework comprises two key components designed to address specific fusion bottlenecks. First, to overcome the information loss caused by radar sparsity, we propose the Radar-assisted Feature Extractor (RFE). By utilizing a physics-aware adaptive diffusion mechanism, RFE densifies radar cues in the region of interest, significantly enhancing detection robustness in adverse weather conditions. Second, to resolve cross-modal spatial misalignment in dynamic scenes, we introduce the Heatmap-guided Feature Aggregation (HFA). This module establishes precise cross-modal correspondence through dual-branch deformable attention,boosting dynamic object detection accuracy by compensating for sensor asynchrony. The framework then performs hierarchical fusion using two specialized components: NFN (Norm-FFN-Norm) for channel-wise feature integration and CBR (Conv-BatchNorm-Relu) for spatial feature enhancement. The proposed RCACF framework is validated on both the nuScenes and View-of-Delft (VoD) datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the baseline method CRN by 1.2% mAP on nuScenes and achieves a 0.39% mAP improvement over the baseline method RCBEVDet on VoD, while having a faster inference speed at 21 ∼ 30 " role="presentation"> 21 ∼ 30 21 ∼ 30 FPS. Published: 2026-04-21T06:52:16+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.788 (must_read)

Authors: Shihao Wang; Keyan Ren; Wenzhuo Zhu; Jingyi Huang; Jianyu Gu; Qing Niu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113778

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Fusing 4D radar and camera data shows greater potential for 3D object detection in autonomous driving systems. However, existing approaches are constrained by static fusion rules and isolated modality processing, leading to limited environmental and inadequate cross-modal complementarity. In an attempt to tackle these problems, we introduce a fast yet effective framework for adaptive and cooperative fusion 4D radar-camera fusion called RCACF. The framework comprises two key components designed to address specific fusion bottlenecks. First, to overcome the information loss caused by radar sparsity, we propose the Radar-assisted Feature Extractor (RFE). By utilizing a physics-aware adaptive diffusion mechanism, RFE densifies radar cues in the region of interest, significantly enhancing detection robustness in adverse weather conditions. Second, to resolve cross-modal spatial misalignment in dynamic scenes, we introduce the Heatmap-guided Feature Aggregation (HFA). This module establishes precise cross-modal correspondence through dual-branch deformable attention,boosting dynamic object detection accuracy by compensating for sensor asynchrony. The framework then performs hierarchical fusion using two specialized components: NFN (Norm-FFN-Norm) for channel-wise feature integration and CBR (Conv-BatchNorm-Relu) for spatial feature enhancement. The proposed RCACF framework is validated on both the nuScenes and View-of-Delft (VoD) datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the baseline method CRN by 1.2% mAP on nuScenes and achieves a 0.39% mAP improvement over the baseline method RCBEVDet on VoD, while having a faster inference speed at 21 ∼ 30 " role="presentation"> 21 ∼ 30 21 ∼ 30 FPS.

item.title: Competing Biases underlie Overconfidence and Underconfidence in LLMs
item.dc:creator: Dharshan Kumaran
Stephen M. Fleming
Larisa Markeeva
Joe Heyward
Andrea Banino
Mrinal Mathur
Razvan Pascanu
Simon Osindero
Benedetto De Martino
Petar Veličković
Viorica Patraucean
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications where reliable confidence estimation is crucial for trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI). However, their confidence dynamics remain poorly understood, with users reporting paradoxical behaviours: LLMs exhibit reduced flexibility in updating initial responses while simultaneously showing excessive sensitivity to contradictory feedback. Understanding these confidence patterns is essential for developing more reliable AI systems and improving human–AI interaction. Here we show that LLM confidence is governed by two competing mechanisms that explain this paradox. First, we identify a choice-supportive bias: when LLMs view their initial answers, they exhibit inflated confidence and maintain their original responses at rates exceeding optimal decision-making, even when presented with contrary evidence. Second, we demonstrate systematic overweighting of contradictory information: LLMs update their confidence more strongly in response to opposing advice than supporting advice, deviating markedly from optimal Bayesian reasoning. These mechanisms operate across diverse models and generalize from simple factual queries to reasoning tasks. Our computational modelling reveals that these two principles—self-consistency preservation and hypersensitivity to contradiction—capture LLM behaviour across domains. These findings provide an understanding of when and why LLMs exhibit adherence to initial responses versus disproportionate updating, with implications for enhancing the robustness and transparency of LLM decision-making. Kumaran et al. show that large language model (LLM) confidence is shaped by two competing biases: a choice-supportive bias that inflates confidence in initial answers, and a systematic overweighting of contradictory advice, deviating from optimal Bayesian reasoning. Published: 2026-04-22T09:06:53+00:00 Venue: Nature Machine Intelligence Score: 0.786 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dharshan Kumaran; Stephen M. Fleming; Larisa Markeeva; Joe Heyward; Andrea Banino; Mrinal Mathur; Razvan Pascanu; Simon Osindero; Benedetto De Martino; Petar Veličković; Viorica Patraucean

Journal: Nature Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1038/s42256-026-01217-9

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications where reliable confidence estimation is crucial for trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI). However, their confidence dynamics remain poorly understood, with users reporting paradoxical behaviours: LLMs exhibit reduced flexibility in updating initial responses while simultaneously showing excessive sensitivity to contradictory feedback. Understanding these confidence patterns is essential for developing more reliable AI systems and improving human–AI interaction. Here we show that LLM confidence is governed by two competing mechanisms that explain this paradox. First, we identify a choice-supportive bias: when LLMs view their initial answers, they exhibit inflated confidence and maintain their original responses at rates exceeding optimal decision-making, even when presented with contrary evidence. Second, we demonstrate systematic overweighting of contradictory information: LLMs update their confidence more strongly in response to opposing advice than supporting advice, deviating markedly from optimal Bayesian reasoning. These mechanisms operate across diverse models and generalize from simple factual queries to reasoning tasks. Our computational modelling reveals that these two principles—self-consistency preservation and hypersensitivity to contradiction—capture LLM behaviour across domains. These findings provide an understanding of when and why LLMs exhibit adherence to initial responses versus disproportionate updating, with implications for enhancing the robustness and transparency of LLM decision-making. Kumaran et al. show that large language model (LLM) confidence is shaped by two competing biases: a choice-supportive bias that inflates confidence in initial answers, and a systematic overweighting of contradictory advice, deviating from optimal Bayesian reasoning.

item.title: LAFI-Diffusion：基于可控扩散模型的卫星遥感舰船目标检测数据集构建与生成方法
item.dc:creator: HE Da
LI Zeyu

item.description: 2026年4月21日国防科技大学 自动目标识别全国重点实验室的胡锡坤团队和中山大学 地理科学与规划学院的何达团队在《遥感学报》发文，介绍了其在卫星遥感图像舰船目标检测与识别领域的研究进展，胡锡坤和何达专家构建了大规模细粒度舰船实例检测数据集LAFI及百万级舰船目标检测数据集LAFI - Diffusion，为突破该领域数据瓶颈提供解决方案。 Published: 2026-04-21T08:59:47+00:00 Venue: National Remote Sensing Bulletin Score: 0.784 (must_read)

item.title: Power Allocation Game Between Multiple Radar System and Jammer for Target Tracking
item.dc:creator: Bo Wang
Gangsheng Zhang
Haowei Zhang
Junwei Xie
Gang Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: This paper investigates a power allocation strategy for target tracking, focusing on the interaction between a multiple radar system (MRS) and a self-defense jammer. A closed-form expression of the Bayesian Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (BCRLB) is derived as a function and employed as the optimization metric. Within a Stackelberg game framework, power allocation models are formulated under two configurations—with the MRS and the jammer, respectively, acting as the leader. In both scenarios, the leader aims to optimize tracking accuracy under a total power budget, while the follower seeks to minimize power consumption while maintaining a predefined tracking performance threshold. To solve this problem, a hybrid iterative algorithm integrating Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions, particle swarm optimization (PSO), and cyclic minimization algorithm is proposed. Furthermore, the existence and uniqueness of the Stackelberg equilibrium are rigorously analyzed. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively balances tracking performance and power consumption. Published: 2026-04-22T19:52:24+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bo Wang; Gangsheng Zhang; Haowei Zhang; Junwei Xie; Gang Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3686334

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

This paper investigates a power allocation strategy for target tracking, focusing on the interaction between a multiple radar system (MRS) and a self-defense jammer. A closed-form expression of the Bayesian Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (BCRLB) is derived as a function and employed as the optimization metric. Within a Stackelberg game framework, power allocation models are formulated under two configurations—with the MRS and the jammer, respectively, acting as the leader. In both scenarios, the leader aims to optimize tracking accuracy under a total power budget, while the follower seeks to minimize power consumption while maintaining a predefined tracking performance threshold. To solve this problem, a hybrid iterative algorithm integrating Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions, particle swarm optimization (PSO), and cyclic minimization algorithm is proposed. Furthermore, the existence and uniqueness of the Stackelberg equilibrium are rigorously analyzed. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively balances tracking performance and power consumption.

item.title: CiviViL: multimodal interpretation of composite urban facility scenes with spatial-semantic-aware bidirectional cross-attention
item.dc:creator: Zhuotong Du
Ning Zhou
Mingting Zhou
Cuili Zhang
Weiyue Shi
Jindi Wang
Qiming Zhou
Haigang Sui
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Urban infrastructure and industrial facilities serve as the major carriers of urban lifeline systems, playing a pivotal role in enhancing urban resilience. These critical facilities are typically complexes composed of multiple components arranged in non-rigid layouts, characterized by form-function decoupling, limited data availability, and a lack of abundant crowd-sourced geospatial information. Existing approaches excel at backbone optimization for general scenes classification but fail to model the complex structural context and intricate relational dependencies essential for interpreting composite urban facility scenes. Though large vision-language models (VLMs) have revolutionized general scene understanding, their potential remains untapped in this domain due to the lack of specialized datasets and mechanisms for encoding domain-specific spatial semantics. To bridge this gap, we construct the first large-scale, fine-grained image-caption multimodal dataset for composite critical facility scenes, comprising 52,603 pairs, and then propose a structured representation method augmented by spatial-semantic decomposition, named CiviViL. Specifically, a vision foundation model is used to extract global image features, while a pre-trained transformer-based language model encodes multi-dimensional discrete spatial contexts associated with the configuration of functional components, including component distribution, facility-component relationships, and view-dependent spatial geometry. A bidirectional cross-attention mechanism (image-to-text and text-to-image) is designed to achieve collaborative reinforcement between visual perception and spatial-relational semantics, enabling the integration of complex associations into a unified scene representation. Extensive experimental results on 13 categories of infrastructure and industrial facilities demonstrate that CiviViL significantly outperforms 21 state-of-the-art baselines, underscoring the effectiveness and superiority of cross-modal collaborative enhancement for fine-grained interpretation of composite urban facility scenes. Published: 2026-04-22T11:07:39+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhuotong Du; Ning Zhou; Mingting Zhou; Cuili Zhang; Weiyue Shi; Jindi Wang; Qiming Zhou; Haigang Sui

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.024

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Urban infrastructure and industrial facilities serve as the major carriers of urban lifeline systems, playing a pivotal role in enhancing urban resilience. These critical facilities are typically complexes composed of multiple components arranged in non-rigid layouts, characterized by form-function decoupling, limited data availability, and a lack of abundant crowd-sourced geospatial information. Existing approaches excel at backbone optimization for general scenes classification but fail to model the complex structural context and intricate relational dependencies essential for interpreting composite urban facility scenes. Though large vision-language models (VLMs) have revolutionized general scene understanding, their potential remains untapped in this domain due to the lack of specialized datasets and mechanisms for encoding domain-specific spatial semantics. To bridge this gap, we construct the first large-scale, fine-grained image-caption multimodal dataset for composite critical facility scenes, comprising 52,603 pairs, and then propose a structured representation method augmented by spatial-semantic decomposition, named CiviViL. Specifically, a vision foundation model is used to extract global image features, while a pre-trained transformer-based language model encodes multi-dimensional discrete spatial contexts associated with the configuration of functional components, including component distribution, facility-component relationships, and view-dependent spatial geometry. A bidirectional cross-attention mechanism (image-to-text and text-to-image) is designed to achieve collaborative reinforcement between visual perception and spatial-relational semantics, enabling the integration of complex associations into a unified scene representation. Extensive experimental results on 13 categories of infrastructure and industrial facilities demonstrate that CiviViL significantly outperforms 21 state-of-the-art baselines, underscoring the effectiveness and superiority of cross-modal collaborative enhancement for fine-grained interpretation of composite urban facility scenes.

item.title: Large vision-language model knowledge guided multi-source urban land-use mapping: A case study of representative cities across six continents
item.dc:creator: Ruiyi Yang
Yu Su
Yinhe Liu
Ailong Ma
Yanfei Zhong
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Urban land-use maps provide critical insights into the functional structure and urbanization process of cities. While high-resolution remote sensing (HRS) images offer detailed information on ground objects, they are insufficient for comprehensive land-use analysis, as land-use patterns involve both natural and socio-economic factors. To address this, integrating HRS images with points of interest (POIs) reflecting human activities has become a common approach. However, variations in physical appearances and POI density across cities pose challenges for large-scale urban land-use mapping. In this study, a large vision-language model knowledge-guided multi-source feature learning framework for urban land-use mapping (VLM-LU) is proposed. By leveraging a large vision-language model (VLM), the framework generates captions robust to environmental changes. It incorporates a voting-based mechanism and rule-based refinement to mitigate VLM hallucinations. Additionally, a joint object-guided visual representation refinement (JGR) module is introduced to improve robustness against variations in urban landscapes by guiding the visual encoder to focus on key areas. A confidence-aware gating (CAG) module further enhances performance by weighting the relative importance of each modality to obtain an informative fused representation. Experimental results on the Multi-CUN dataset and representative cities across six continents demonstrate that the proposed VLM-LU framework significantly improves the generalizability and robustness of large-scale urban land-use mapping. In several study areas, the overall accuracy (OA) of VLM-LU exceeds state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by more than 10%. Even with limited annotated samples, VLM-LU still performs well, demonstrating its potential practicality in large-scale land-use mapping. The source code is available at https://github.com/Rayoll/VLM-LU . Published: 2026-04-21T19:18:36+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (must_read)

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.004

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Urban land-use maps provide critical insights into the functional structure and urbanization process of cities. While high-resolution remote sensing (HRS) images offer detailed information on ground objects, they are insufficient for comprehensive land-use analysis, as land-use patterns involve both natural and socio-economic factors. To address this, integrating HRS images with points of interest (POIs) reflecting human activities has become a common approach. However, variations in physical appearances and POI density across cities pose challenges for large-scale urban land-use mapping. In this study, a large vision-language model knowledge-guided multi-source feature learning framework for urban land-use mapping (VLM-LU) is proposed. By leveraging a large vision-language model (VLM), the framework generates captions robust to environmental changes. It incorporates a voting-based mechanism and rule-based refinement to mitigate VLM hallucinations. Additionally, a joint object-guided visual representation refinement (JGR) module is introduced to improve robustness against variations in urban landscapes by guiding the visual encoder to focus on key areas. A confidence-aware gating (CAG) module further enhances performance by weighting the relative importance of each modality to obtain an informative fused representation. Experimental results on the Multi-CUN dataset and representative cities across six continents demonstrate that the proposed VLM-LU framework significantly improves the generalizability and robustness of large-scale urban land-use mapping. In several study areas, the overall accuracy (OA) of VLM-LU exceeds state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by more than 10%. Even with limited annotated samples, VLM-LU still performs well, demonstrating its potential practicality in large-scale land-use mapping. The source code is available at https://github.com/Rayoll/VLM-LU .

item.title: PDDA: Prompt-driven Domain Adaptation for Real-world Image Dehazing
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhang
Yuxin Feng
Xin Li
Fan Zhou
Zhuo Su
item.description: Due to the complexity and diversity of practical environments, real-world image dehazing remains an unresolved problem, with one of the key challenges being how to bridge the distribution gap between synthetic and real domains. This paper proposes a Prompt-driven Domain Adaptation (PDDA) framework within the bi-level optimization perspective. Specifically, we introduce hyperparameter optimization-based bi-level modeling: the lower-level optimization emphasizes prior learning within the synthetic domain to stabilize dehazing performance, while the upper-level optimization focuses on enhancing cross-domain adaptability to ensure that the model can generalize across different domains. Given the scarcity of paired real haze images, we train learnable haze prompts by jointly optimizing the text-image similarity between positive/negative prompts and corresponding clear/haze images in the CLIP latent space to more effectively capture real-world haze characteristics. Based on the learned haze prompts, we construct an unsupervised cross-domain loss function that enhances the adaptability to complex real-world scenarios by integrating prompt learning with bi-level optimization strategy. Furthermore, we conduct a comprehensive exploration to uncover the inherent properties of PDDA, including architecture-irrelevant flexibility and domain-agnostic robustness. Extensive experiments across a wide range of benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves both quantitative and qualitative improvements across diverse scenarios, showing robust performance not only in real-world daytime conditions but also exhibiting superior cross-domain adaptation capabilities in nighttime scenarios. Codes are available at https://github.com/YanZhang-zy/PDDA.git. Published: 2026-04-21T19:51:35+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.782 (must_read)

Authors: Yan Zhang; Yuxin Feng; Xin Li; Fan Zhou; Zhuo Su

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3684402

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Due to the complexity and diversity of practical environments, real-world image dehazing remains an unresolved problem, with one of the key challenges being how to bridge the distribution gap between synthetic and real domains. This paper proposes a Prompt-driven Domain Adaptation (PDDA) framework within the bi-level optimization perspective. Specifically, we introduce hyperparameter optimization-based bi-level modeling: the lower-level optimization emphasizes prior learning within the synthetic domain to stabilize dehazing performance, while the upper-level optimization focuses on enhancing cross-domain adaptability to ensure that the model can generalize across different domains. Given the scarcity of paired real haze images, we train learnable haze prompts by jointly optimizing the text-image similarity between positive/negative prompts and corresponding clear/haze images in the CLIP latent space to more effectively capture real-world haze characteristics. Based on the learned haze prompts, we construct an unsupervised cross-domain loss function that enhances the adaptability to complex real-world scenarios by integrating prompt learning with bi-level optimization strategy. Furthermore, we conduct a comprehensive exploration to uncover the inherent properties of PDDA, including architecture-irrelevant flexibility and domain-agnostic robustness. Extensive experiments across a wide range of benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves both quantitative and qualitative improvements across diverse scenarios, showing robust performance not only in real-world daytime conditions but also exhibiting superior cross-domain adaptation capabilities in nighttime scenarios. Codes are available at https://github.com/YanZhang-zy/PDDA.git.

item.title: Prompting Foundation Models for Zero-Shot Ship Instance Segmentation in SAR Imagery
item.dc:creator: Islam Mansour
Francescopaolo Sica
Michael Schmitt
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) plays a critical role in maritime surveillance, yet deep learning for SAR analysis is limited by the lack of pixel-level annotations. This paper explores how general-purpose vision foundation models can enable zero-shot ship instance segmentation in SAR imagery, eliminating the need for pixel-level supervision. A YOLOv11-based detector trained on open SAR datasets localizes ships via bounding boxes, which then prompt the Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) to produce instance masks without any mask annotations. Unlike prior SAM-based SAR approaches that rely on fine tuning or adapters, our method demonstrates that spatial constraints from a SAR-trained detector alone can effectively regularize foundation model predictions. This design partially mitigates the optical-SAR domain gap and enables downstream applications such as vessel classification, size estimation, and wake analysis. Experiments on the SSDD benchmark achieve a mean IoU of 0.637 (89% of a fully supervised baseline) with an overall ship detection rate of 89.2%, confirming a scalable, annotation-efficient pathway toward foundation-model-driven SAR image understanding. Published: 2026-04-20T07:57:11+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Islam Mansour; Francescopaolo Sica; Michael Schmitt

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) plays a critical role in maritime surveillance, yet deep learning for SAR analysis is limited by the lack of pixel-level annotations. This paper explores how general-purpose vision foundation models can enable zero-shot ship instance segmentation in SAR imagery, eliminating the need for pixel-level supervision. A YOLOv11-based detector trained on open SAR datasets localizes ships via bounding boxes, which then prompt the Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) to produce instance masks without any mask annotations. Unlike prior SAM-based SAR approaches that rely on fine tuning or adapters, our method demonstrates that spatial constraints from a SAR-trained detector alone can effectively regularize foundation model predictions. This design partially mitigates the optical-SAR domain gap and enables downstream applications such as vessel classification, size estimation, and wake analysis. Experiments on the SSDD benchmark achieve a mean IoU of 0.637 (89% of a fully supervised baseline) with an overall ship detection rate of 89.2%, confirming a scalable, annotation-efficient pathway toward foundation-model-driven SAR image understanding.

item.title: IR-SPC:Image Restoration with Starting Point Controlled Diffusion Model
item.dc:creator: Cheng Yang
Lijing Liang
Shuo Liu
Lei Zhang
Zhixun Su
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: This paper proposes a starting point controlled (SPC) diffusion model for general-purpose image restoration, which directly models the image degradation process as a diffusion process. The key idea of the SPC approach is to transform a high-quality image into a degraded counterpart, which is a low-quality image with added Gaussian noise as the starting point for the reverse process rather than pure Gaussian noise. Then, by simulating the corresponding SPC reverse-time process, we can restore the original high-quality image from its low-quality image without requiring any task-specific prior knowledge. Crucially, the IR-SPC method has a closed-form solution, allowing us to compute the true posterior probability and learn it with a neural network. Furthermore, we introduce a maximum likelihood objective to optimize the reverse trajectory that stabilizes the training and improves the restoration results. Additionally, we also propose optimized training and sampling algorithms to improve the computational efficiency and algorithmic consistency of the IR-SPC framework. Extensive experimental results show that our proposed method achieves highly competitive performance in quantitative comparisons of perceptual metrics, setting a new state-of-the-art perception metrics on restoration tasks. Published: 2026-04-22T19:52:08+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cheng Yang; Lijing Liang; Shuo Liu; Lei Zhang; Zhixun Su

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3686668

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

This paper proposes a starting point controlled (SPC) diffusion model for general-purpose image restoration, which directly models the image degradation process as a diffusion process. The key idea of the SPC approach is to transform a high-quality image into a degraded counterpart, which is a low-quality image with added Gaussian noise as the starting point for the reverse process rather than pure Gaussian noise. Then, by simulating the corresponding SPC reverse-time process, we can restore the original high-quality image from its low-quality image without requiring any task-specific prior knowledge. Crucially, the IR-SPC method has a closed-form solution, allowing us to compute the true posterior probability and learn it with a neural network. Furthermore, we introduce a maximum likelihood objective to optimize the reverse trajectory that stabilizes the training and improves the restoration results. Additionally, we also propose optimized training and sampling algorithms to improve the computational efficiency and algorithmic consistency of the IR-SPC framework. Extensive experimental results show that our proposed method achieves highly competitive performance in quantitative comparisons of perceptual metrics, setting a new state-of-the-art perception metrics on restoration tasks.

item.title: PMNet: Parametric manifold network for infrared small target detection
item.dc:creator: Ruoqi Lian
Shangwei Deng
Ziqian Chen
Qianwen Ma
Haofeng Hu
Xiaobo Li
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains a challenging task due to the low signal-to-noise ratio and significant background interference, which make it difficult to preserve fine target details. Conventional neural networks, constrained by their local receptive fields, often struggle to separate complete target details from high-frequency noise across channels, resulting in suboptimal performance. To address this limitation, we propose a parametric manifold network, i.e., PMNet, that integrates frequency-domain analysis with manifold learning. Based on the wavelet decomposition, PMNet separates high-frequency features from the spatial domain to encode high-frequency target details across channels; accordingly, the manifold-aware module, built upon parametric uniform manifold approximation and projection, captures non-local cross-channel high-dimensional features. This design effectively preserves the geometric structure of the target while suppressing noise, thus achieving more accurate edge and contour segmentation. Additionally, a low-frequency feature block is introduced to refine structural features, enhancing discrimination between targets and backgrounds. Extensive experiments on three IRSTD datasets demonstrate that the PMNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness, highlighting the effectiveness of integrating wavelet decomposition with manifold learning in IRSTD. Open-source implementations will be available at https://github.com/Lianruoqi/PMNet . Published: 2026-04-22T23:01:44+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.780 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruoqi Lian; Shangwei Deng; Ziqian Chen; Qianwen Ma; Haofeng Hu; Xiaobo Li

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113828

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains a challenging task due to the low signal-to-noise ratio and significant background interference, which make it difficult to preserve fine target details. Conventional neural networks, constrained by their local receptive fields, often struggle to separate complete target details from high-frequency noise across channels, resulting in suboptimal performance. To address this limitation, we propose a parametric manifold network, i.e., PMNet, that integrates frequency-domain analysis with manifold learning. Based on the wavelet decomposition, PMNet separates high-frequency features from the spatial domain to encode high-frequency target details across channels; accordingly, the manifold-aware module, built upon parametric uniform manifold approximation and projection, captures non-local cross-channel high-dimensional features. This design effectively preserves the geometric structure of the target while suppressing noise, thus achieving more accurate edge and contour segmentation. Additionally, a low-frequency feature block is introduced to refine structural features, enhancing discrimination between targets and backgrounds. Extensive experiments on three IRSTD datasets demonstrate that the PMNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness, highlighting the effectiveness of integrating wavelet decomposition with manifold learning in IRSTD. Open-source implementations will be available at https://github.com/Lianruoqi/PMNet .

item.title: DFDNet: Differential Fusion Detection Network for RGB-IR Objects
item.dc:creator: Liming Zhou
Yuanfei Xie
Jiakang Yang
Guochong Zhang
Yi Xie
Yadi Wang
Xianyu Zuo
item.description: Visible-infrared (RGB-IR) target detection provides an effective all-weather solution for robust perception in complex environments. However, existing methods lack sufficient adaptability to the physical property differences between modalities during feature extraction, making it challenging to fully exploit complementary information. Meanwhile, most cross-modal fusion mechanisms suffer from limited expressive capacity or parametric redundancy. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a differential fusion detection network (DFDNet). The core contribution of this work is to depart from the homogenized feature extraction paradigm of existing methods by constructing a dual-branch-specific feature extraction architecture tailored to the physical properties of each modality. For the infrared modality, we design a conditional routing convolution (CRConv) and construct a thermal-kinetic feature extraction (TKFE) module. This module dynamically adapts to the thermal radiation characteristics of different objects, significantly enhancing the representation of critical thermal targets. For the visible modality, we propose a multi-granularity visual representation (MVR) module, which captures diverse visual features ranging from detailed textures to macroscopic silhouettes, thereby enhancing the discriminative properties of object shapes and textures. Furthermore, to efficiently integrate the heterogeneous features extracted from these specialized branches, we propose a collaborative weight decision (CWD) module. Instead of employing complex interactions like previous methods, this module adopts a dynamic weight prediction mechanism. It enables adaptive feature fusion with a parameter count comparable to a simple concatenation operation, achieving an excellent balance between efficiency and effectiveness. Extensive experiments on the VEDAI, DroneVehicle, and FLIR Aligned datasets validate the effectiveness of DFDNet. It achieves mAP@0.5 scores of 73.4%, 82.3%, and 75.4%, respectively, outperforming many existing methods by a significant margin. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively leverages the complementary advantages of RGB and IR modalities to achieve robust and efficient target detection. Published: 2026-04-22T15:28:09+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Liming Zhou; Yuanfei Xie; Jiakang Yang; Guochong Zhang; Yi Xie; Yadi Wang; Xianyu Zuo

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132494

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Visible-infrared (RGB-IR) target detection provides an effective all-weather solution for robust perception in complex environments. However, existing methods lack sufficient adaptability to the physical property differences between modalities during feature extraction, making it challenging to fully exploit complementary information. Meanwhile, most cross-modal fusion mechanisms suffer from limited expressive capacity or parametric redundancy. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a differential fusion detection network (DFDNet). The core contribution of this work is to depart from the homogenized feature extraction paradigm of existing methods by constructing a dual-branch-specific feature extraction architecture tailored to the physical properties of each modality. For the infrared modality, we design a conditional routing convolution (CRConv) and construct a thermal-kinetic feature extraction (TKFE) module. This module dynamically adapts to the thermal radiation characteristics of different objects, significantly enhancing the representation of critical thermal targets. For the visible modality, we propose a multi-granularity visual representation (MVR) module, which captures diverse visual features ranging from detailed textures to macroscopic silhouettes, thereby enhancing the discriminative properties of object shapes and textures. Furthermore, to efficiently integrate the heterogeneous features extracted from these specialized branches, we propose a collaborative weight decision (CWD) module. Instead of employing complex interactions like previous methods, this module adopts a dynamic weight prediction mechanism. It enables adaptive feature fusion with a parameter count comparable to a simple concatenation operation, achieving an excellent balance between efficiency and effectiveness. Extensive experiments on the VEDAI, DroneVehicle, and FLIR Aligned datasets validate the effectiveness of DFDNet. It achieves mAP@0.5 scores of 73.4%, 82.3%, and 75.4%, respectively, outperforming many existing methods by a significant margin. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively leverages the complementary advantages of RGB and IR modalities to achieve robust and efficient target detection.

item.title: Navigating Score Matching within Diffusion Models
item.dc:creator: Junyu Zhang
Daochang Liu
Shichao Zhang
Chang Xu
item.description: Diffusion models (DMs) have achieved remarkable generative performance, revolutionizing advancements in computer vision. Unfortunately, image quality is affected by inaccurate scores when the sampling trajectory gets stuck in low-density regions between disjoint modes, as these areas suffer from a scarcity of training data. To alleviate this, we put forward a hybrid generative-discriminative training framework designed to develop score direction navigation abilities. Specifically, our framework simultaneously optimizes both tasks using the given noisy input images, enabling the score to learn its direction by distinguishing its corresponding cluster mode. In this context, during sampling, the improved score guides the sampling trajectory through the high-density regions of its corresponding mode, thus avoiding inaccurate scores. Following this design philosophy, image quality can be naturally improved through the learned directional navigation abilities, without affecting sampling diversity. Extensive experimental results on four widely used datasets and various diffusion model architectures demonstrate the effectiveness and cost-efficiency of our framework. Published: 2026-04-21T19:50:08+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Junyu Zhang; Daochang Liu; Shichao Zhang; Chang Xu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3685791

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Diffusion models (DMs) have achieved remarkable generative performance, revolutionizing advancements in computer vision. Unfortunately, image quality is affected by inaccurate scores when the sampling trajectory gets stuck in low-density regions between disjoint modes, as these areas suffer from a scarcity of training data. To alleviate this, we put forward a hybrid generative-discriminative training framework designed to develop score direction navigation abilities. Specifically, our framework simultaneously optimizes both tasks using the given noisy input images, enabling the score to learn its direction by distinguishing its corresponding cluster mode. In this context, during sampling, the improved score guides the sampling trajectory through the high-density regions of its corresponding mode, thus avoiding inaccurate scores. Following this design philosophy, image quality can be naturally improved through the learned directional navigation abilities, without affecting sampling diversity. Extensive experimental results on four widely used datasets and various diffusion model architectures demonstrate the effectiveness and cost-efficiency of our framework.

item.title: Self-supervised pretraining for an iterative image size agnostic vision transformer
item.dc:creator: Nedyalko Prisadnikov
Danda Pani Paudel
Yuqian Fu
Luc Van Gool
item.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) dominate self-supervised learning (SSL). While they have proven highly effective for large-scale pretraining, they are computationally inefficient and scale poorly with image size. Consequently, foundational models like DINO are constrained to low-resolution processing. A recent foveal-inspired transformer achieves resolution agnosticism by iteratively processing a fixed-size context of multi-zoom patches. This model demonstrated promising results via supervised learning, utilizing a sequential, recurrent-like process without backpropagation through time. To unlock its potential as a foundational backbone, we introduce a novel sequential-to-global SSL framework based on DINO's self-distillation objective. Supported by an efficient integral-image patch extraction method, our approach enables large-scale pretraining for image-size agnostic vision encoders. We achieve competitive performance on ImageNet-1K and downstream classification tasks, maintaining a constant computational budget regardless of input resolution. Published: 2026-04-22T09:53:28+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nedyalko Prisadnikov; Danda Pani Paudel; Yuqian Fu; Luc Van Gool

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Vision Transformers (ViTs) dominate self-supervised learning (SSL). While they have proven highly effective for large-scale pretraining, they are computationally inefficient and scale poorly with image size. Consequently, foundational models like DINO are constrained to low-resolution processing. A recent foveal-inspired transformer achieves resolution agnosticism by iteratively processing a fixed-size context of multi-zoom patches. This model demonstrated promising results via supervised learning, utilizing a sequential, recurrent-like process without backpropagation through time. To unlock its potential as a foundational backbone, we introduce a novel sequential-to-global SSL framework based on DINO's self-distillation objective. Supported by an efficient integral-image patch extraction method, our approach enables large-scale pretraining for image-size agnostic vision encoders. We achieve competitive performance on ImageNet-1K and downstream classification tasks, maintaining a constant computational budget regardless of input resolution.

item.title: Geo-R1: Improving few-shot geospatial referring expression understanding with reinforcement fine-tuning
item.dc:creator: Zilun Zhang
Zian Guan
Tiancheng Zhao
Haozhan Shen
Yuxiang Cai
Zhonggen Su
Yongheng Shang
Zhaojun Liu
Jianwei Yin
Xiang Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Referring expression understanding in remote sensing poses unique challenges, as it requires reasoning over complex object–context relationships. While supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) achieves strong performance with massive labeled datasets, they struggle in data-scarce scenarios, leading to poor generalization. To address this limitation, we propose Geo-R1, a reasoning-centric reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) paradigm for few-shot geospatial referring. Geo-R1 can generate explicit, interpretable reasoning chains that decompose referring expressions, and then leverage these rationales to localize target objects, which provides great interpretability. We validate Geo-R1 on three carefully designed few-shot geospatial referring benchmarks, where our model consistently and substantially outperforms SFT baselines. It also demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization, highlighting its robustness. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/Geo-R1/geo-r1 . Published: 2026-04-22T07:25:58+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zilun Zhang; Zian Guan; Tiancheng Zhao; Haozhan Shen; Yuxiang Cai; Zhonggen Su; Yongheng Shang; Zhaojun Liu; Jianwei Yin; Xiang Li

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.023

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Referring expression understanding in remote sensing poses unique challenges, as it requires reasoning over complex object–context relationships. While supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) achieves strong performance with massive labeled datasets, they struggle in data-scarce scenarios, leading to poor generalization. To address this limitation, we propose Geo-R1, a reasoning-centric reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) paradigm for few-shot geospatial referring. Geo-R1 can generate explicit, interpretable reasoning chains that decompose referring expressions, and then leverage these rationales to localize target objects, which provides great interpretability. We validate Geo-R1 on three carefully designed few-shot geospatial referring benchmarks, where our model consistently and substantially outperforms SFT baselines. It also demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization, highlighting its robustness. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/Geo-R1/geo-r1 .

item.title: AlignMLLA: A Lightweight Dual-stage Alignment Fusion Network for Infrared-visible Image Fusion
item.dc:creator: Lijun Yuan
Ming Yang
Qiqin Li
Chuanjiang Xie
Xinyu Zhu
item.description: Visible-infrared (RGB-IR) fusion for object detection is prone to instability in small-object and low-contrast scenarios due to local geometric misalignment and global distribution shift. To mitigate these effects, a detection baseline is constructed to retain modality-specific information via dual-parallel RGB-IR feature extraction, with a direction-attention partial C2f (DA-PC2f) embedded in the IR branch to strengthen shallow thermal-detail representation at negligible cost. On this baseline, a lightweight dual-stage alignment fusion network (AlignMLLA) is proposed: local token-level alignment is first performed via Optimal Transport to correct geometric mismatch, and global distribution alignment loss is then enforced during training by applying a Maximum Mean Discrepancy to mitigate modality shift. The aligned unimodal features are subsequently fused by a Mamba-Like Linear Attention design with confidence-aware gating. Extensive experiments on three representative datasets demonstrate that, relative to state-of-the-art multimodal approaches, our method provides a more favorable efficiency-accuracy trade-off: competitive detection performance is achieved while the parameter count and computational cost are reduced by up to 94% and 92%, respectively. Moreover, compared with the strongest lightweight unimodal baseline YOLOv12n, the mean average precision (mAP) is increased by 1%–3%. The source code is available at https://github.com/yuan95766-spec/AlignMLLA . Published: 2026-04-21T23:27:49+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lijun Yuan; Ming Yang; Qiqin Li; Chuanjiang Xie; Xinyu Zhu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113800

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Visible-infrared (RGB-IR) fusion for object detection is prone to instability in small-object and low-contrast scenarios due to local geometric misalignment and global distribution shift. To mitigate these effects, a detection baseline is constructed to retain modality-specific information via dual-parallel RGB-IR feature extraction, with a direction-attention partial C2f (DA-PC2f) embedded in the IR branch to strengthen shallow thermal-detail representation at negligible cost. On this baseline, a lightweight dual-stage alignment fusion network (AlignMLLA) is proposed: local token-level alignment is first performed via Optimal Transport to correct geometric mismatch, and global distribution alignment loss is then enforced during training by applying a Maximum Mean Discrepancy to mitigate modality shift. The aligned unimodal features are subsequently fused by a Mamba-Like Linear Attention design with confidence-aware gating. Extensive experiments on three representative datasets demonstrate that, relative to state-of-the-art multimodal approaches, our method provides a more favorable efficiency-accuracy trade-off: competitive detection performance is achieved while the parameter count and computational cost are reduced by up to 94% and 92%, respectively. Moreover, compared with the strongest lightweight unimodal baseline YOLOv12n, the mean average precision (mAP) is increased by 1%–3%. The source code is available at https://github.com/yuan95766-spec/AlignMLLA .

item.title: MAFFUNet: A Multimodal Attention and Feature Fusion Framework for Remote Sensing Image Integration
item.dc:creator: Songjia Zhang
Yi Lin
Qingli Li
Xiaonan Yang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Multimodal image fusion in remote sensing, integrating data from sources like SAR, optical, multispectral, and hyperspectral sensors, is critical for applications such as land cover classification and disaster monitoring. However, challenges like modality heterogeneity, cloud occlusion, and cross-domain variability demand advanced fusion algorithms capable of capturing nonlinear correlations and ensuring robust generalization.We propose MAFFUNet, a lightweight U-Net-based framework with a five-layer encoder-decoder architecture, incorporating depthwise separable convolutions for efficiency and temporal data support via flattening. MAFFUNet integrates three novel modules: Adversarial Feature Fusion (AFF) for maximizing mutual information, Hybrid Attention and Multiscale Fusion (HAMF) for optimizing spectral and spatial features, and Cross-Domain Regularization (CDR) for enhancing cross-domain adaptability.Experiments on the Chikusei, PaviaC, and PaviaU datasets at 4 times times , 8 times times , and 16 times times scaling factors demonstrate that MAFFUNet outperforms traditional methods and deep learning approaches in metrics like RMSE, PSNR, ERGAS, and SAM, with superior spectral fidelity and spatial detail preservation. Its low computational complexity suits resource-constrained environments.MAFFUNet provides an efficient and robust solution for multimodal remote sensing image fusion. Published: 2026-04-21T19:49:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.774 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
